preamble: The elision of [h] is a universal sound change often referred to as 'H-dropping'. It marks the end-stage of lenition where the glottal fricative loses all articulatory force and disappears entirely.

examples.note: /h/ is often elided between voiced sounds in rapid speech (e.g., 'anhye' [anye]).
PMP *h is lost in most positions in standard Indonesian/Malay; e.g., *həman (name) → nama. Some dialects preserve /h/ intervocalically.
The initial Latin H was lost in early Vulgar Latin.
Common feature of Cockney and Estuary English.
